data.title: What Warren Buffett Saw In Occidental Petroleum -- And Why The Thesis Still Holds
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Occidental Petroleum (OXY) is a best-in-class Permian operator whose proprietary CO2 enhanced oil recovery techniques offer a durable technical edge over peers. The company's recently streamlined balance sheet following the OxyChem divestiture leaves the company well-positioned to generate meaningful free cash flow at conservative oil prices. At a long-run assumption of $75/barrel, owner earnings of approximately $5.30/share imply an intrinsic value of $40–$45, a figure that does not require an optimistic commodity outlook and embeds substantial optionality from rising EOR recovery rates and DAC carbon credit sales. The primary risk is a structural oil supply glut driven by new South American production, but investors with a multi-year horizon and comfort with commodity exposure are buying Berkshire(BRK.B)-backed management with a technologically-advanced producer focused on the prolific Permian basin.

Business Model

Oxy is the third largest domestic oil & gas producer with a big position in the Permian basin. The company is unique in their advanced EOR (enhanced oil recovery) procedures in which they employ proprietary CO2 flooding techniques, which push successive waves of water and CO2 through porous shale rock to improve recovery rates. In conventional (meaning non-shale) wells oil companies typically get ~75% of oil out of the reservoir using CO2 flooding, but that number stands at just 10% in shale. Oxy is the industry leader in this practice, and as one of the largest holders of non-conventional acreage in America, it stands to benefit from continued advances in this technology.

Oxy’s production is based primarily in the US (84% of 2025 MBoe), with domestic revenue split ~85%/10%/5% between oil/NGLs/natural gas. US-based production mostly comes from the Permian (65%), with the remainder from the DJ basin (24%) and Gulf of America (11%).

International production splits looks similar, with 2025 sales split between 80% oil, 10% NGLs, and 10% natural gas from projects in Oman, the UAE, and Oman.

Bottom line – Oxy’s financial performance is largely dependent on their American production, specifically from the Permian. One disadvantage of Permian production is the relatively low in-basin price of oil and gas, with the Waha basis commonly pulling the price of natural gas below $0. Oxy’s midstream segment is designed to alleviate this local oversupply by transporting product to US refineries and to the gulf for export.

Oxy captures these transportation spreads (Midland-to-GC oil and Waha-to-GC gas) through their marketing group (housed within the midstream segment). This part of the business purchases pipeline capacity to the gulf and optimizes the utilization with their own product and by purchasing from third parties. Oxy doesn’t explicitly own much pipeline takeaway capacity beyond their own gathering systems.

Much of the physical midstream infrastructure Oxy does own is wrapped up in their 43% ownership of a publicly traded MLP – WES – which owns ~15k miles of pipeline plus associated treatment/compression facilities. WES distributed about $600 million to Oxy in 2025.

The physical midstream infrastructure Oxy explicitly owns is focused on wet-gas treatment and CO2 extraction/delivery. Oxy’s facilities purify the gas stream coming out of wells to produce pipeline-quality gas, extract NGLS, and – unique to Oxy – capture CO2 for delivery back to wells to be used in continuing CO2 flooding operations.

Oxy Low Carbon Ventures (OLCV) is also housed within the midstream segment and is in the process of developing direct-air CO2 capture (DAC) technologies. The STRATOS DAC facility is expected to begin capturing and sequestering CO2 this year and will eventually generate revenue from carbon removal credit sales to customers like JP Morgan (JPM) and Palo Alto Networks (PANW). The appeal of DAC is apparent given Oxy’s CO2 expertise, but this has been a cost center for several years and I do not factor prospective earnings from this venture in my analysis.

Recent Developments

To understand Oxy, you need to understand the series of acquisitions and divestitures that have led to today’s form. Oxy purchased Anadarko for $55 billion in 2019 with backing from Berkshire Hathaway in the form of $10 billion in preferred stock (with an 8% yield) plus warrants to purchase 83.9 million shares at ~$60/share. Ironically – the company’s market cap today is roughly equivalent to the price they paid for Anadarko.

Still, Berkshire’s backing was a strong vote of confidence, which was important as Oxy significantly levered their balance sheet. Between 2018 and 2019 LT debt jumped from $10 billion to over $38 billion and the share count was significantly diluted as a result of Berkshire’s warrants (diluted shares outstanding are up about 20% from 2018 to today). The company sold off its African assets to Total along with some other non-core American acreage to bring down their heavy debt load.

While this massive acquisition at a high price initial smacks of empire building, Warren Buffett’s comments on the transaction are reassuring:

In the case of Occidental specifically, they'd had an analyst presentation... I read it over a weekend. And what Vicki Hollub was saying made nothing but sense. And I decided that it was a good place to put Berkshire's money... Vicki was saying what the company had gone through and where it was now and what they planned to do with the money... But we decided it made sense. And two weeks later, we had 14% of the company.

Since that transaction, Oxy has made two additional major transactions with the same strategic focus – a $12 billion acquisition of Permian E&P Crown Rock and the divestiture of their OxyChem unit to Berkshire for $9.7 billion. Oxy had reduced their debt load to ~$17 billion in 2023 when they announced the Crown Rock acquisition, but obviously had to increase leverage to swallow the new addition. Their recent Oxychem divestiture gets the company where it originally wanted to be debt-wise (around $15 billion with no significant maturities through 2029) with a focused portfolio of Permian acreage where the company feels like they have a significant advantage through their EOR techniques.

Earnings Power and Valuation

So where does this series of transactions leave them now? The table below shows recent results and my 2026 estimate of production and earnings power.

My production assumptions for 2026 are based on a combination of Q4, 2025 daily production rates and management guidance for 2026. I've projected an average sales price of $75/barrel for oil, $18/barrel for NGLs and $1.60/Mcf for natural gas. Oxy’s marketing team uses midstream capacity to get average sales prices as close to market prices as possible, but the company is more focused on oil than natural gas, which explains why their historical sales price for oil is closer to the WTI index than natural gas is to Henry Hub. My calculation of owner earnings is simply net income + DD&A – capex, which conservatively includes growth capex.

The resulting earnings power of ~$5.30/share (which includes additional dilution from Berkshire exercising all warrants) should be fairly conservative in that it doesn’t incorporate significant production increases, much growth in oil/gas prices, or any benefit from DAC sales and the ramp-down of capex as the Stratos DAC facility nears completion in 2026.

Under these assumptions, with earnings growth of just 3%/year in perpetuity, the value of Oxy’s owner earnings discounted at a rate of 15% (representing my opportunity cost) are around $40-$45. This leaves significant upside in the form of higher commodity prices, CO2 sequestration sales, and improved recovery rates from Oxy’s world-class EOR techniques. Furthermore, the recent spike in oil prices from the war in Iran shows how Oxy may benefit from an increasingly turbulent world.

Risks

The biggest risk to an investment in Oxy is a structural oversupply in the oil market. Prior to the Iranian war, the world was awash in oil. That dynamic has been temporarily broken by the current state in the Strait of Hormuz, but it should fundamentally keep a lid on oil prices over the next several years. New discoveries in Brazil, Guyana, and Argentina along with expanded production in Venezuela may eventually lessen the impact from middle-eastern turbulence and eventually worsen the supply glut. For that reason, it’s probably best not to bank on sustained high commodity prices as an investment thesis for Oxy, despite the current state of affairs.

Another risk of investing in Oxy is further share dilution from large acquisitions. Vicki Hollob has shown that she’s not scared of making large purchases, obtaining financing by any means necessary. Further high-priced acquisitions combined with low oil & gas prices would be a poor environment for future shareholder returns.

Finally, in a capital-intensive operation such as Oxy’s, higher interest rates present a significant risk. As mentioned above, the company has no major debt maturities before 2029, which counteracts this risk in the near-term, but may impact them as debt rolls over into the 2030s.

Summary

Occidental Petroleum is a high-quality Permian operator with a differentiated position in CO2-enhanced oil recovery, a strategically focused asset base following its Anadarko and Crown Rock acquisitions, and a now-cleaned-up balance sheet after the OxyChem divestiture. Investors comfortable with commodity price volatility and willing to own a leveraged, Permian-concentrated E&P are buying a company with genuine technical advantages, Berkshire’s continued backing, and a valuation that does not require an optimistic oil price outlook to justify.

I’m personally interested in become a long-term shareholder, but will be staying on the sidelines for now. The company was trading within my target price range in the low-$40s prior to the Iran war, which has catapulted the company’s stock price well above $50. If the war resolves within the next few weeks (as seems likely), Oxy’s stock price may drift back down into a range I’m more comfortable buying.

data.title: A Prudent Investor Watches What Insiders Do, Not What They Say
data.description:
The market has been hit by elevated volatility here in March due to the conflict in Iran which has taken nearly 20% of global oil supplies offline. However, every dip in equities has been bought and the major indexes are not that far off from their all-time highs.

"Whoever is careless with the truth in small matters cannot be trusted with important matters” ― Albert Einstein

This leaves stocks still trading at extreme valuation levels, using numerous historical valuation metrics. I have always been a proponent of judging an individual by their actions, not their words. And prudent investors should be watching the 'insiders' closely for clues on where markets might head from here. In today's article, I will highlight some observations I have on corporate insiders and managers of investor funds over the past couple of months.

We will start with some positives. Kudos to the management of KKR & Co. (KKR). The CEO and a couple of insiders have gobbled up just over $35 million in new equity purchases in recent weeks. The company has seen some of the negative headlines as brethren Blue Owl Capital (OWL), BlackRock (BLK) and Jefferies Financial Group (JEF), all of which have been hit by deteriorating conditions in the credit markets. Nonetheless, the stock is down 40% from its highs late last summer. The insider buying is one of few signs of conviction I have seen from insiders in recent months.

In addition, one has to give credit to Blackstone (BX). Company managers threw in $150 million of their own money and the firm offered up $250 million of its own capital to allow redemptions of 7.9% at one large fund instead of the stated five percent quarterly cap last week.

This action contrast positively to those of Blue Owl Capital and BlackRock which have had some high profiled 'gating' of investor redemption requests in recent weeks, which have sent ripples across the credit markets. And the environment for the private credit space just has continued to deteriorate this week.

Wednesday, JPMorgan Chase (JPM) ⁠disclosed Wednesday that it has marked ​down ‌the value ‌of ⁠certain loans ⁠held by private-credit groups. The huge bank also is tightening credit and limiting funding to the sector. It also became known Wednesday that Cliffwater was seeing higher than seven percent redemption requests around its flagship $33 billion private credit fund.

Also, on Wednesday, the president at PIMCO, which has $2.3 trillion AUM, did his best Captain Obvious impression and stated that 'the rising strain in the private credit market is a result of years of sloppy underwriting standards in lending'. Which is a bit like closing the barn door after most of the horses have escaped. All of this tells me is we are going to see many more 'cockroaches' in the private credit markets in the months and quarters ahead.

Before the 'SaaSpocalypse' along with growing worries around AI disruption and new concerns around private credit in February and a new conflict in the Middle East, insiders were growing wearier around market valuations. The insider selling/buying ratio hit its highest levels in five years during January. Of course, this was happening just as the retail investor was pushing margin debt to all-time highs.

Finally, just as private credit funds are facing spiking redemption requests, some well-known billionaires are trying to go public so they can sell stakes in their firms at heightened valuations. Bill Ackman's Pershing Square is seeking to go public. Elon Musk's SpaceX is reportedly exploring an IPO in mid-2026 at a valuation of up to $1.5 trillion. This would be the largest IPO in history.

If this IPO actually gets executed, it well could be like ringing the bell at the top. It will be a fascinating development to watch. In the meantime, I will continue to monitor the 'tea leaves' coming from investors. And right now, they are reinforcing my cautious view on the overall markets.

data.title: Accenture: Undervalued After Years Of Overvaluation
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

How easy is it for you to forgive somebody?

I mean truly forgive someone, as in forgive them and move on without constantly mentioning it or using it as ammo over their head. If we were honest with ourselves, the vast majority of us really struggle with forgiveness. We're good at saying the words. We're bad at living it out afterward. We like to hold grudges, to constantly bring up past failures to beat down someone who's trying to move forward, or use those past failures as leverage to force this person to do something they don't want to do.

When it comes to investing, most investors are no better. There is a reason why the dividend cutters have statistically worse performance over the long run than those companies that initiate dividends or grow dividends. It's because investors are unforgiving. Aside from maybe some high-yield investors who see dividend cuts regularly and continue to buy or recommend the same companies over and over. Most investors are not willing to play with fire when it comes to their capital.

What happens if a company hasn't been cutting its dividend, but just has become extremely expensive? Investors shouldn't just look at dividend growth and dividend yield to determine the value of a company. They need to look at what the company has traded hands for historically versus what it's trading hands for now. Investors can often get frustrated when they buy an overvalued company, and then that company's value returns from the sky back to earth. Many investors struggle with what I call "Icarus syndrome." I'll release an article in the future specifically discussing what this is. But in short, Icarus Syndrome is when we fly too high, whether we're buying too high of yields or too high valuations, and we suffer the consequences when we fall back to Earth.

As an investor with decades of investment time left on my horizon and with most of my readers being investors with decades of time left on their horizon, whether they're in the midst of their retirement, which can be upwards of 30 years long if you live to a standard lifespan, or whether they're still working and saving for retirement, which means they have even more time on the clock, buying overvalued securities can guarantee underperformance in the long run. When a company is no longer overvalued, it becomes time to reacquaint yourself with it.

So let's get familiar with the company that has finally come back to Earth.

A Dividend Aristocrat In The Making

Employing over 780,000 people and serving more than 9,000 clients in 120 countries, Accenture (ACN) is the world's largest professional services firm. The company handles critical business processes for clients, including marketing and sales, human resources, supply chain, and finance and accounting.

ACN's industry dominance is built on its top 10 ecosystem partners, which generated more than 60% of total revenue in Q1 2026. That includes hyperscalers (AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud) and enterprise platforms (SAP, Oracle, Salesforce, and ServiceNow). These strategic alliances allow it to co-create solutions rather than just resell them.

ACN is committed to keeping its lead, too. According to CFO Angie Park's opening remarks during the Q1 2026 Earnings Call, the company expects to allocate about $3 billion to acquisitions in FY 2026. In January, ACN announced the latest deal: an agreement to acquire Faculty, a leading UK-based AI native services and products business. Upon closing, this deal will expand ACN's capabilities to help clients reinvent core business processes with secure AI solutions that drive tangible outcomes.

The company's low cost of capital and healthy balance sheet are what support these acquisitions. To this point, ACN enjoys an AA- S&P credit rating with a stable outlook.

Financially, the company is a cash flow machine ($2.3 billion in Q1 2026 share repurchases and $1 billion of dividends paid). In Q1 2026, ACN posted $2.2 billion in advanced AI bookings. That was a blistering 76% year-over-year growth rate. This proved that while discretionary consulting is slowing, the AI pivot is pushing adjusted operating margins higher (+30 basis points YOY to 17.0% in Q1 2026).

Analysts are upbeat, too. The FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for adjusted EPS to compound by 8.2% annually through FY 2028, off a FY 2025 base of $12.93.

ACN's 3.4% dividend yield is also the highest it has been in over a decade. Better yet, the adjusted EPS payout ratio is set to be in the mid- to high-40% range for FY 2026. That provides it with plenty of flexibility to keep handing out 10% annual dividend hikes for the foreseeable future (for a Chowder number of approximately 13). This would enable ACN to build on what's already a 15-year dividend growth streak.

ACN's shares are arguably a compelling value right now. At the current $201 share price, the stock is priced at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of just 15.2x. Put into perspective, this is far below both the FAST Graphs 20-year average of 21.3 and the 10-year average of 25.7.

We would argue that a rational fair value P/E ratio for ACN is 20. That's because its 8.2% annual adjusted EPS growth consensus isn't too far off the 10-year CAGR of 10.4%.

This suggests that ACN's growth profile is largely intact. Taking out recent years (2021 through 2024) that inflated the stock's valuation multiple, we believe that our fair value multiple properly considers the risks. This indicates shares of ACN are trading at a 33% discount to our $280 fair value per share estimate.

If the company lives up to growth projections and reverts to fair value, it could post a 49% total return through February 2027. By the end of FY 2031, ACN could deliver 17% annual total returns.

Of course, ACN isn't without its risks. Still, we think these are more than priced in at current valuation levels. Key risks for ACN include an inability to attract/retain top-notch talent and a pause on digital transformation projects.

The company's continued success largely depends on its ability to keep its employees with market-leading skills and capabilities. If ACN's corporate culture can't hire or upskill employees to keep pace with the industries it serves and technological changes, it may not deliver the services needed to fulfill client demand. That could materially alter its growth potential.

Additionally, many enterprises are pausing multi-year enterprise resource planning or cloud migration projects that were once considered essential. This is based on the idea that AI-native versions could soon be available that do it cheaper.

ACN is responding to this environment by bundling smaller and faster AI projects into a broader strategic narrative to keep contract values high. In the meantime, that has at least temporarily pressured its growth. The contrarian take, though, is that these spending freezes can't persist forever because companies will eventually realize that they can't scale AI on decades-old data foundations.

Conclusion

ACN has fallen back to Earth because of the SaaS Apocalypse and surrounding growth sell-off. Yet as a fundamental business, ACN is extremely healthy. Not only can they reward shareholders with growing dividends that are now at a decade-high yield compared to their past, but they're also actively buying back their shares. The deeper ACN falls, the larger impact its share repurchases will be able to have, the more shares they're able to repurchase. Any EPS growth will be magnified due to the limited number of shareholders that remain thereafter. Share buybacks do not create new value inside the company. They create value for each individual shareholder who remains thereafter. And I like share buybacks as an effective means to reward shareholders so long as the company is doing it at an attractive value and isn't actively working on issuing new shares in the near term. There's no point in buying back your shares here if you're just going to issue new shares tomorrow. However, if you're able to buy back shares from excess cash flow while simultaneously growing your dividend, enabling you to have more dividend growth in the future, it's a win-win for everyone involved.

When it comes to being a dividend investor? Who is focused on buying strong value proposition opportunities and growing dividends? It's important to be willing to let a company come off the bench once it's no longer overvalued. What we've done today is take a look at ACN, which has been on the bench for being overvalued for a long time, but now has fallen back into an attractive price range once more to be able to provide strong total returns and a rebound as the market turns. AI is not going away. AI is likely not going to destroy everything that you know, just like personal computers didn't. But AI is going to rewrite some industries and boost productivity in other places, and ACN is an investment that allows you to enjoy the rewards of that future. Those rewards can help build your financial fortress to new strengths and new heights.

Become a Dividend King. Sit in your dividend castle.

data.title: February CPI Inflation: Steady State Can Anchor Future Rise
data.description:
As CPI inflation stays steady at both the headline and core level for February, at any other time the release would be no news. But with a war underway, the focus is not on what has happened, but what's coming.

Higher prices in March are a given, going by the recent oil price shock. It's clear that they leave no room for monetary easing later this month. Stock markets are understandably bearish too. But there are still investing opportunities around. All of this is discussed below.

What the February CPI inflation report says

CPI inflation remained unchanged from January at 2.4% YoY and core inflation remained at 2.5% YoY as well. While there's zero development as a result from last month, these are positive outcomes anyway in that they provide some room for an inflation increase without causing any particular economic disruption. Consider their context as follows:

  • The headline figure for January 2026 was at a nine month low, which means that the level is sustained in the latest read.
  • Both headline and core inflation figures are lower than the average monthly inflation over the past 12 months of 2.6% YoY and 2.8% YoY respectively.
  • The figures are also exactly at the Fed's target rate for PCE inflation for 2026.

Why the inflation increase can be muted

In any case, the inflation increase might be rather muted even as Brent crude futures have more than doubled since the start of the year. Here's how:

  • Oil prices seen subsiding: The US's EIA has upped its fuel price forecasts recently, with the rise in Brent spot price expected to be 37% bigger (see table below) to USD 79/bbl now. However, with Brent trading at over USD 90/bbl right now, the updated forecast clearly expects that the oil price spike won't sustain. In fact, the EIA says that it expects Brent's price to drop to USD 70/bbl by the end of 2026.
  • Base effect on oil price forecast: In 2025, crude price averaged at USD 69/bbl, while the initial forecast for 2026 put the number 16% lower at USD 58/bbl. As a result, while the forecast has now jumped by 37%, compared to last year, the expected increase is now a smaller 15%. Similarly, retail gas price is now expected to rise by 7.7% compared to a 6.1% drop forecast earlier. This is an important distinction to note since inflation is observed on a YoY basis.
  • Limited impact of crude price increase: One rule of thumb to assess the impact of crude price rise on inflation is that a USD 10/bbl uptick in crude price can increase inflation by 0.2 percentage points. Now, with the average increase in Brent's price expected to be just USD 10/bbl YoY, the uptick in inflation wouldn't wind up being that big. Even considering that higher gas prices and second round effects of fuel prices would also lend upward pressure on inflation, it's safe to assume that the increase won't be more than 0.5 percentage points. So, if inflation were to average at 2.4% otherwise, it can now rise to 2.9%. This is just slightly higher than the 2.7% average level seen in 2025.

No rate cuts in sight

It's clear though, that the inflation figure would get farther away from the Fed's comfort level of 2%. As a result, if the reserve stays true to form, it's going to turn hawkish once again in its FOMC statement due next week.

After all, fuel prices still remain elevated and the Fed has said more than once that inflation expectations need to be anchored (see here and here). As fuel prices recede, it might have more room for dovishness. But for now, it's little wonder that the markets see less than 1% chance of a rate cut (see chart below).

With economic projections due to release with the FOMC statement as well, however, we could anticipate an increase in inflation expectations. The Fed's expected policy rate for the year could change in tandem, which will give insight on what to expect over the remainder of the year.

Negative market response

All three key stock indices, the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX, SPY), the NASDAQ 100 Index (NDX) (QQQ) and the Dow Jones Industrial Average Index (DJI) corrected in yesterday's session (see table below). This was unlikely a response to the inflation report, though. It was more likely an ongoing response to the geopolitical situation underway. It's worth highlighting here that the S&P 500 has corrected in five of the eight sessions in March so far.

Where to invest now

So, right now we have a situation where markets are diffident and inflation is expected to rise just enough to keep policy rates unchanged. At the same time, rise in prices is unlikely to be high enough to cripple the economy. This indicates that cyclical equity sectors like financial services and consumer discretionary, which are the worst impacted YTD by 8.6% and 4.2% respectively (see chart below), could well have overcorrected.

I believe it's exactly these stocks that can see an uptick if the market environment begins to improve, as would be expected if the situation in the Middle East calms down. So, here are a few investing ideas:

  • Cyclical sector ETFs: First, the sectors' ETFs can be considered, with some care though, as they don't have unanimous Buy ratings. The State Street Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF) gets a Buy rating from Seeking Alpha analysts but is rated Sell by Quant Rating. The State Street Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Income ETF (XLY), gets an underwhelming Hold rating from both Seeking Alpha analysts and Quant Rating.
  • Financial services stocks: The biggest five holdings under XLF, all get Buy ratings from Seeking Alpha analysts as well as Wall Street. Four of these, JP Morgan Chase & Co (JPM), Visa (V), Mastercard (MA) and Bank of America (BAC) have also seen corrections of over 10%+ each YTD. These could be good stocks to consider buying on the dip.
  • Consumer discretionary stocks: For consumer discretionary stocks, I like Royal Caribbean Cruises (RCL), which is among the top 10 holdings in XLY. It's rated Buy by both Seeking Alpha and Wall Street analysts, which also coincides with my rating. Amazon (AMZN), the biggest holding in XLY, also gets a Buy rating from analysts, but I'm less certain of a potential uptick, at least in the short-term, having downgraded it to Hold recently.

What next?

If the current expectations of a softening in fuel prices over the rest of the year come to pass, inflation could stay largely under control going by its recent stability. This in turn, can improve the market's mood as well, resulting in a rotation back to cyclical equity sectors like financial services and consumer discretionary.

It could be a good time then to buy high quality cyclical stocks. Even if the situation worsens for now, over time, they have a good chance of bouncing back.

data.title: Return Of The Pawn Shop - Ezcorp And The Cascading Credit Cycle
data.description:
EZCORP Overview and Market Opportunity

EZCORP (EZPW) is an owner and operator of 1,383 pawn shops in the U.S. and Latin America, offering consumers access to short-term cash by using personal items as collateral. The company makes money through interest payments on loans made to consumers and the eventual sale of collateralized items that went unclaimed. EZCORP has become more favorable among consumers facing higher interest rates and credit issues, as the company does not require credit checks, bank accounts, and/or other verifications. More recently, EZCORP has engaged in strategic transactions to expand its pawn store offerings. EZCORP acquired Founders One, which adds 105 stores operating under the "La Familia Pawn and Jewelry" and "CashWiz" brands across 12 countries.

Q1 FY 2026 Earnings Report

In Q1 FY 2026, EZCORP reported total revenues of $374.5M, compared to $320M in Q1 FY 2025. Much of this increase was attributed to higher scrap, merchandise sales, and pawn service charges (PSC). While the rise of precious metal prices had bolstered company revenues, EZCORP's Latin America growth has also been very impressive. Management reported an 18% YoY increase in PSC, driven by same-store PLO growth and new stores.

The company also reported EBITDA of $70.3M, up 36% YoY, with an EBITDA margin of 19%, increasing by 260 bps YoY. This strong performance has bolstered EZCORP's balance sheet; short-term cash & cash equivalents as of December 31, 2025 were ~$466M. This totally ignores the company's acquisition of Founders One, as the transaction closed after the balance sheet date. However, I don't expect management to slow its acquisition strategy as it looks to use idle cash to grow its Latin American pawn markets, which have seen stronger growth than U.S. markets.

EZCORP Buy Thesis

While many investors may think that the recent price spike in the precious metals market is the only catalyst to EZCORP, I see a broader unfolding of a worsening credit environment and strategic M&A as remaining the key catalysts to continued stock outperformance. The company currently trades at a ~9.68x EV/EBITDA multiple. Additionally, EZCORP's momentum largely parallels its strength during the 2008 financial crisis.

EZCORP's shares have increased from strong revenue growth through store expansion and increased precious metal transactions, but remain below 2008 levels. The current credit environment for consumers remains very expensive, with the U.S. commercial bank interest rate on credit cards at ~21%, which is well above the 14.5% from peak 2008 levels. As the unemployment rate continues to rise, I expect demand for alternative credit instruments, which include short-term loans by pawning items, to increase.

Deteriorating Consumer Credit Supporting Pawn Shop Cycle

Despite a growing economy and strong stock market gains in recent years, the consumer credit environment continues to worsen, with delinquency rates on loans (including CCs and mortgages) rising to 4.8% of all outstanding US household debt in Q4 2025. Credit card loans were even worse, with 12.7% of these outstanding loans listed as 90-day delinquent, which is the most since Q1 2011. With a growing unemployment rate and stickier inflation more likely, I don't see these delinquency rates improving soon.

While total debt in default remains well below 2008 levels, EZCORP shares remain elevated on the back of strong earnings growth.

EZCORP's Effective Profit Reinvestment Cycle

While recent performance has been bolstered by precious metals, management is keen on using its recent earnings windfall to fuel CapEx and return cash to shareholders through share buybacks. Full-year FY 2025 EBITDA was ~$181.46M; this operating strength has been reflected in the company's strong balance sheet, which holds $466M in cash & cash equivalents. Management has signaled its interest to reinvest its earnings. In January 2026, EZCORP reported the acquisition of El Buffalo Pawn, which operates 12 pawn stores in Texas, for approximately $27.5M. This acquisition was alongside the majority investment in Founders One.

Management also approved a new share repurchase program for $50M, which followed its previous repurchase program of another $50M. The total impact of repurchases would help reduce the total share count by ~3%, using the current equity value of EZCORP's shares.

EV/EBITDA Multiples Remain Attractive

Despite strong share price performance in 2025, EZCORP's EV/EBITDA levels remain below historical levels during strong financial performance. Ignoring the 2016 multiple expansion when the company was struggling financially, EV/EBITDA is still below 2008 levels.

In EZCORP's peak operating FY, which was 2012, the company generated $222M in EBITDA (FactSet historical data). Given the infancy of this credit cycle and the expectation of worsening credit for consumers, forecasted EBITDA should far exceed historical levels. In FY 2026, analysts (aggregated from FactSet) expect full-year EBITDA of $245M.

Valuation - Expanding EV/EBITDA

Expecting that EZCORP continues to parallel its 2008 stock price rise, I expect investor optimism on EBITDA to grow faster than expected, which would ultimately raise EV/EBITDA multiples. Assuming an 11x EV/EBITDA multiple, which would be below the company's peak multiple during the financial crisis, shares should be trading ~13% higher than current levels. This share price appreciation is in addition to personal optimism on EZCORP's growth in sales and net income as consumer credit options continue to worsen in the United States.

Other Considerations (Risks)

Seasonality of Precious Metals

EZCORP generates profits from jewelry scrap sales, which jumped 129% due to higher precious metals prices. Changes in the price of these precious metals are independent of the company's core operating business segment and may create short-term volatility in EZCORP's sales and profit.

Foreign Exchange Risks

Many of EZCORP's stores are located in Latin America, which exposes the company to foreign currency risk. Short-term changes in these exchange rates can substantially impact the company's bottom line and make it harder to forecast income in future periods.

EZCORP Buy Conclusion

As the credit environment for consumers continues to weaken, dampened by sticky inflation and historically high credit card interest rates, I expect EZCORP to continue to outperform analyst estimates. The company's cyclical strength during the 2008 financial crisis indicates that current share prices have plenty of room to run. Peak operating performance came four years after the 2008 financial crisis. While macro analysis represents forecasting risk, the parallels of the credit environment to 2008 and EZCORP's rising share price are too hard to ignore. Therefore, EZCORP is a buy.

data.title: Oil Shock For Asia: Identifying The Key Pressure Points
data.description:
By Deepali Bhargava, Regional Head of Research, Asia-Pacific

Where the pressure points are likely to emerge

A key question for policymakers right now is how quickly rising oil prices will begin to strain Asian economies. Under a scenario where supply disruptions last for a month and then gradually ease throughout the year, we expect Brent crude oil (CO1:COM) to average US$83/bbl, about $15/bbl higher than the 2025 baseline.

Our previous work on import exposures shows that Thailand and Korea carry the largest oil and gas trade deficits in Asia, and are therefore most exposed to supply shocks and price spikes. Taiwan, the Philippines, Singapore and India follow closely, facing meaningful but more varied vulnerabilities depending on domestic buffers and pricing policies.

Below, we discuss how the impact might differ across the region, shaped by each country’s energy mix, import dependencies, domestic fuel buffers and fiscal capacity to absorb subsidy costs.

Asia’s fragile energy buffers

Asia’s energy resilience is uneven; while Japan and Korea can absorb shocks for longer, thinner LPG buffers and heavy LNG dependence leave several economies exposed. Those with limited reserves, particularly Indonesia, could face the sharpest pressure if supply risks continue to escalate.

Energy reserve adequacy varies sharply across Asia. Japan holds the deepest cushion, with reserves covering 254 days of domestic demand, followed by South Korea at 210 days. India maintains about 74 days, while the Philippines keeps close to two months' worth of supply across refined products.

While oil inventories appear broadly sufficient in the near term, LPG buffers remain notably thin, increasing vulnerability to price spikes and supply disruptions. With LNG prices up nearly 70% since the flare‑up, economies heavily reliant on imported gas - Thailand, Korea, and Japan - face outsized exposure to further volatility.

At the other end of the spectrum, Indonesia’s reserves cover only about 25 days, placing it among the most at‑risk economies should disruptions persist into the second quarter.

A substitution cushion: Coal dependence helps India and China

A key differentiator across the region is the scope of fuel substitution. India and China benefit from a built‑in shock absorber because more than half of their energy supply still comes from coal. Although both remain net coal importers, their ability to substitute oil with coal at the margin provides an important cost advantage. The contrast in recent market moves is stark: coal prices have risen only about 12% since the conflict began, compared with an approximate 70% surge in natural gas. This divergence gives India and China a meaningful substitution buffer that could reduce their exposure to oil and gas price spikes to some extent.

By contrast, Korea has limited room to substitute. Its high dependency on both oil and gas leaves it exposed, even though the government has temporarily capped domestic petroleum prices. A weakening Korean won further amplifies imported inflation, prompting us to raise our 2026 CPI forecast by 0.2ppt to 2.2%, with upside risks if Brent remains above $80/bbl.

If the government continues to cap retail prices without offering subsidies, refiners will have to absorb the widening cost gap, which may not be a feasible solution for the medium term.

Tolerance levels: Singapore and Taiwan have the deepest fiscal cushion

Asian governments are responding very differently to higher crude prices, reflecting differences in subsidy systems, fiscal buffers and political willingness to pass costs through. Several economies - including Indonesia, Japan and India - are prioritising price stability and have held retail fuel prices unchanged despite rising global benchmarks, relying on state‑owned oil companies or fiscal support to cushion consumers. By contrast, Singapore and the Philippines have already raised pump prices by 10-13% since March 2, signalling a deliberate policy choice to allow earlier pass-through.

If disruptions escalate, Singapore and Taiwan appear best positioned to sustain higher oil prices, supported by relatively strong fiscal positions, healthier current account dynamics and greater capacity for targeted support. India also has meaningful tolerance, with state-owned oil companies able to absorb losses until crude exceeds $130/bbl. This has kept petrol and diesel prices stable so far, although the government has adjusted LPG prices upwards.

Indonesia, however, faces a far tighter constraint. Officials have indicated a “worst-case scenario” of crude averaging $92/bbl, well above the $70/bbl assumption in the 2026 budget - highlighting limited room to manage sustained shocks without broader fiscal strain. The Philippines has even less of a buffer: domestic gasoline prices rose 5% last week, with another 12% increase announced, underscoring its rapid pass-through, narrow subsidy capacity and higher sensitivity to global price swings.

The Philippines: Fastest pass-through, higher near-term risk

The Philippines is likely to feel higher oil prices sooner than most Asian counterparts, such as Thailand or Indonesia, given its modest fuel buffers, rapid domestic price pass-through and a structurally wider current account deficit. As one of the region’s most oil-dependent economies, alongside Thailand, Korea, Vietnam and Singapore, a sustained rise in crude has meaningful external implications. The strain is already visible, with several local governments shifting to four-day workweeks in response to the recent surge in fuel costs.

Inflation dynamics will depend on how quickly higher energy costs spill over into transport, electricity and food. The 2022 episode - when inflation jumped from 4% to 8.5% as oil averaged around $100/bbl - showed how sensitive the economy is to simultaneous increases in fuel and food prices, especially when the currency depreciates. Conditions today are somewhat more stable, with rice supply from Vietnam intact and agricultural availability generally adequate. However, forward risks remain. Fertiliser prices could rise if Middle East supply tightens, eventually pressuring yields and food prices later in the year - a meaningful vulnerability given the Philippines’ exposure to imported food and fuel.

Overall, we are increasing our current account deficit forecast for the Philippines to 4% of GDP in 2026. A $15/bbl increase in Brent crude could widen the Philippines’ current account deficit by around 0.7% of GDP, assuming stable demand. This would increase the risk of renewed peso weakness if elevated oil prices persist. In our scenario of sustained oil disruptions for a month, CPI inflation for the Philippines is expected to inch closer to the upper end of 4% of the BSP’s target range.

Market impact

While Singapore, Korea, and Taiwan benefit from sizeable current account surpluses that help absorb higher import costs, Thailand’s surplus is far more modest, leaving it with less of a buffer. Meanwhile, India and the Philippines stand out as the only major economies running structural current account deficits, though India has kept its gap contained at roughly 1% of GDP in recent years.

Foreign exchange reserve adequacy adds another layer to the risk profile. Malaysia, Indonesia, and South Korea hold comparatively lower FX cover relative to their import needs, making them more exposed to a prolonged rise in crude prices. In contrast, the Philippines and India maintain stronger FX buffers, giving their central banks greater capacity to manage currency pressures if oil-related outflows intensify.

Philippines: Given the upward revision to our CPI forecasts, we no longer expect the BSP to cut rates this year. Persistently higher oil prices also pose a risk of further delaying the recovery in GDP growth, which is already starting from a muted base. We maintain our 2026 GDP forecast at 5.2%, with a meaningful upturn expected only in the second half of the year. We anticipate weak growth pressures to persist in the first half of 2026 at least, as ongoing investigations and unresolved political and oil price uncertainty continue to weigh on both business confidence and broader economic sentiment.

India: Oil price risks remain manageable for now, as oil marketing companies are absorbing the increase in crude costs without passing them through to retail prices. As a result, we are keeping our CPI inflation forecast unchanged and continue to expect inflation to average below the RBI’s medium-term target of 4% in 2026. However, the INR remains vulnerable, as higher crude prices are likely to widen the current account deficit and exert additional pressure on the currency.

Indonesia: As a net exporter of oil and gas, Indonesia typically benefits from higher global oil prices; however, already strained fiscal balances risk further deterioration as the government increases subsidies on retail fuel. The widening fiscal deficit has already weighed on the IDR, and further deterioration could amplify this pressure.

At the same time, Indonesia’s real rate differential versus the US has narrowed sharply - by more than two percentage points between November 2025 and February 2026 - adding to currency headwinds. Compounding this, sizeable foreign investor outflows from government debt since September 2025 and weakening appetite for Indonesian equities in 2026 have further weighed on the IDR, and with investor caution likely to keep FII inflows subdued, we expect additional depreciation pressure ahead.

With relatively lesser pass-through of higher oil prices to retail prices for petroleum products, we are keeping our CPI inflation forecast unchanged at 2.6%. Given the soft domestic growth outlook, we believe BI’s easing cycle is not yet complete. Once the currency stabilises, further rate reduction remains likely to support growth. We currently expect two additional 25bp cuts in the first half of 2026, though the risks are skewed toward a delay.

Singapore: Given the expected pass-through from higher crude oil prices to domestic pump prices, we are raising our 2026 Singapore headline CPI inflation forecast from 1.8% to 2.0%. Singapore has emerged as a standout performer in Asia, with GDP growth in 2025 significantly surpassing expectations; 4Q25 growth accelerated to 6.9% YoY, driven by a sharp rebound in manufacturing, particularly pharmaceuticals and electronics. The city-state has been a clear beneficiary of AI-related demand and has maintained competitiveness through its relatively low tariff exposure, supporting robust export performance - a trend we expect to persist. Against this backdrop of strong growth and firming inflation, we continue to anticipate that the Monetary Authority of Singapore will tighten its FX policy stance in April. Singapore’s strong fiscal position and substantial current account surplus should also help limit depreciation pressures on the currency.

In conclusion, higher oil prices will strain Asia unevenly, with Thailand, the Philippines and Korea facing the earliest pressure due to weak buffers, fast pass-through and sizeable import dependence, respectively. India and China are better cushioned by coal substitution, while Singapore and Taiwan benefit from strong fiscal and external positions.

Market risks are most acute where reserves are thin and current account deficits widen - particularly the Philippines and Indonesia.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: Smith Douglas: Upgrade To Strong Buy
data.description:
Update Since My Prior Article

In my Jan. 7, 2026, article comparing Smith Douglas Homes Corp. (SDHC) with Green Brick Partners, Inc. (GRBK), I rated Smith Douglas a cautious Buy, noting that while the company possessed an efficient operating model and strong revenue growth, its valuation appeared somewhat rich relative to Green Brick at the time.

Since then, the housing market has remained under pressure from elevated mortgage rates and persistent affordability challenges, and Smith Douglas’ most recent earnings call confirmed that the industry is currently operating in what management described as a “recessionary housing environment” that has lasted roughly 18 months.

The company’s Q4 2025 earnings results (see the full transcript here) reflected these headwinds. Smith Douglas delivered 780 homes in the fourth quarter, generating $260 million in revenue, but revenue declined 9% year-over-year. Gross margin fell to 19.9%, down from 25.5% in the same quarter of the prior year, primarily due to increased buyer incentives and closing cost assistance used to address affordability concerns.

For the full year 2025, the company delivered a record 2,908 homes, representing a 1% increase in closings, while revenue remained essentially flat at $971 million. However, profitability declined meaningfully as incentives increased: pretax income fell from $116.9 million in 2024 to $70.9 million in 2025, and adjusted net income declined from $88.1 million to $53.5 million.

Management made it clear that the margin pressure is largely the result of a deliberate strategy to prioritize “pace over price,” meaning the company is willing to accept lower margins to maintain sales velocity and keep its production engine running.

Despite the challenging environment, several operational metrics remain mildly encouraging. Net new home orders increased 3% year-over-year to 2,726 homes, and the company’s active community count expanded 28% to 100 communities. Smith Douglas also ended the year controlling approximately 22,300 lots, the vast majority through option contracts consistent with its land-light strategy, and maintained a very conservative balance sheet with only 9% debt-to-capitalization.

In short, the earnings call unmistakably confirms that profitability is under pressure due to sector and macroeconomic headwinds. Meanwhile, the stock price has fallen to around $12 per share as of this writing, substantially below the roughly $17 level when I initiated my moderate Buy rating.

A Quick Recap Of Smith Douglas’ Competitive Advantages

As discussed in my January 2026 article, Smith Douglas operates under the asset-light, land-light strategy, that is, rather than owning large land banks, the company controls most of its lots through option contracts.

Operationally, the company has demonstrated exceptional efficiency. Its build cycle of slightly under 60 business days is considerably shorter than the roughly six-month sector average, allowing the company to maintain a high asset turnover rate to achieve higher utilization of its capital.

While Smith Douglas earns lower margins per home than vertically integrated builders like Green Brick, it partially compensates through speed and volume, building and selling homes much faster than virtually all of its publicly traded competitors.

This competitive advantage appears to remain intact and promises to deliver sector-beating investment returns to its patient long-term investors, at least for those investors who have the stomach to buy and accumulate shares on days of gloom and doom such as today, as opposed to buying them during cyclical highs such as Y2024 when the same stock was traded around $36, or about thrice today's price.

The Latest Earnings Call: Margin Pressure But Operational Discipline

The company’s most recent earnings call confirmed that the US overall housing environment remains challenging. Management acknowledged that demand conditions have been “choppy” and that affordability pressures continue to weigh on buyers.

In response, the company has adopted a strategy that management described as “pace over price.” In other words, Smith Douglas is willing to provide potential buyers with price concessions and other incentives, which will translate into short-term profit margin compression in order to maintain sales velocity and keep its production engine humming.

This strategy, if properly executed, is probably the most rational among all imperfect choices that the management has in a cyclical downturn. Unloading the inventories as fast as possible at prices consistent with the prevailing market conditions allows the company to put capital back to work, keep its workforce engaged, and position itself to benefit when housing demand eventually improves. Of course, providing too many and too substantial concessions may compromise the pricing power and even the reputation of the company in the long term.

Importantly, despite these difficult conditions, the company still managed to increase closings during 2025, a notable achievement when many builders across the industry experienced declining volumes.

Insider Buying Sends A Strong Signal

What I did not pick up in my January 2026 article was the significant insider buying that occurred in May 2025. As detailed in Seeking Alpha, several senior insiders and directors purchased shares in the open market, including the founder and chairman of the company:
